But in C++, we need to initialize the counts array. Let's code:

class Solution {
public:
    bool digitCount(string num) {
        vector<int> counts(10, 0);
        for (char c : num) {
            int d = c - '0';
            counts[d]++;
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < num.length(); ++i) {
            int expected = num[i] - '0';
            if (counts[i] != expected) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};

Now, time and space complexity.

Time complexity: O(n), where n is the length of the string. We loop through the string once to count, then loop through the indices once (O(n) again). So overall O(n).

Space complexity: O(1) because the counts array is fixed size (10 elements), regardless of n. Even though 10 is a small fixed number, so space is O(1).

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    bool digitCount(string num) {
        vector<int> counts(10, 0);
        for (char c : num) {
            int d = c - '0';
            counts[d]++;
        }
        for (int i = 0; i < num.length(); ++i) {
            int expected = num[i] - '0';
            if (counts[i] != expected) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a fixed-size array to count occurrences of each digit in the input string. It then verifies each index's requirement by comparing the precomputed counts with the expected values from the string. This approach ensures linear time and constant space complexity.
